text: The surgeon continues to dissect the retrosternal space, gradually expanding the view to reveal more of the anatomical structures within this area.
The surgeon cuts a portion of the diaphragm to expand the surgical field, providing a wider view of the retrosternal space.
The surgeon carefully deflects tissue from the middle, avoiding tearing tiny blood vessels during the thymectomy procedure.
The surgeon opens the right pleural cavity and places a 5mm additional port in the fifth intercostal space.
The surgeon achieves a clear view of the right phrenic nerve, enhancing operability within the surgical field.
The surgeon begins dissection of the right epiphramic fat pads, carefully separating them from the diaphragm and pericardium.
The surgeon checks the left phrenic nerve and determines the resection line on the caudal side, preparing for further dissection.
The surgeon lifts the thymic tissue off the pericardium, exposing the junction of the right and left brachiocephalic veins.
The surgeon pulls the thymus tissue towards the caudal side and dissects the right superior horn along with the surrounding fat tissue using a light gasser V held in the left hand.
The surgeon uses forceps and a Maryland dissector through an additional port to achieve an ideal dissection angle.
The surgeon uses a ligature device to seal the mid-veins, ensuring hemostasis during the thymectomy procedure.
The surgeon identifies the left superior horn of the thymus and prepares to safely divide it, ensuring clear visualization of the anatomical structure.

The surgical field provides a clear view of the right brachiocephalic artery, trachea, left common carotid artery, and right brachiocephalic vein, with these anatomical structures prominently visible.
The surgeon removes the thymectomy specimen in a plastic bag through the sub-xiphoid incision, completing the extraction process.
